title: Tea on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Tannin (polyphenolic). Tea contains theaflavins and thearubigins (oxidized catechin polymers) plus tannin polyphenols. Black tea stains more than green tea due to higher oxidation. The polyphenols bind to fiber through hydrogen bonding with cellulose hydroxyl groups. On cotton (cellulose fiber): tannin pigments bond through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. Over time, tannin oxidizes and polymerizes, shifting from bright (reversible) to brown (resistant). Treatment: acidic tannin formula to break initial hydrogen bonds, followed by H₂O₂ 6% to cleave chromophore conjugation.
whyThisWorks: Tea gets its color from concentrated plant pigments that bind tightly and darken over time. Cotton absorbs liquids quickly and holds onto stains deep in the weave. An oxygen-based treatment breaks apart the pigment molecules, removing the color rather than just spreading it around.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin and tamp gently. This acidic spotter targets the bonds that coffee, wine, tea, and fruit juice pigments form with fabric. Work from the edge toward the center. Check progress every few minutes.
2. Apply H₂O₂ 6% to the stain. Monitor carefully — check every 5 minutes. On colored fabrics, test an inconspicuous area first.
3. Flush with NSD and a mild leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin pigment and any excess oxidizer. Rinse thoroughly.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to stop any remaining oxidizer activity and restore the fabric to its natural state. Blot dry and air dry completely.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — hot water sets tannin stains permanently
warnings: Using hot water on this stain — sets it permanently
Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

steps: 1. Blot the stain with a clean white cloth to soak up as much as you can.
2. Apply the cleaning solution (1 tbsp dish soap + 1 cup 3% hydrogen peroxide (brown bottle from pharmacy)) and let it sit for 5–10 minutes.
3. Blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the color fades.
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution.
5. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try an oxygen bleach (OxiClean) soak — apply and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Use a white cloth to avoid transferring dye from a colored cloth.
Speed matters — fresh tannin stains lift much more easily than dried ones.

neverDo: Use ammonia, baking soda, or alkaline agents — they permanently darken tannin stains
Apply heat before stain is completely gone
Use hot water — sets tannin color faster
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates peroxide well. For colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam before use. High heat sets tannin permanently.

escalation.when: Tannin has cross-linked with the fiber polymer after two professional-grade treatments
escalation.whatToTell: Tea stain on cotton, tannin-based. Treated at home with H₂O₂ and NSD. Please use professional tannin formula and oxidizer protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this black tea, green tea, or herbal tea?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black tea stains more deeply than green or herbal tea due to higher oxidation levels. Tea with milk adds a protein component. Dryer exposure sets the stain significantly. On colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam first.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates the solutions.
Dab the tea stain immediately with cool water applied to a white cloth. Work from center outward to prevent spreading.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Tea contains tannins that oxidize and turn brown — act quickly to prevent setting.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply detergent/vinegar solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 2 oz white vinegar + 2 oz cool water) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon from center outward.
Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working from center outward.
Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% mixed with ammonia (equal parts) and allow to sit for 3 minutes to oxidize tannin residue.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Test on hidden area first — peroxide may lighten some dyes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply white vinegar to neutralize and remove any rings. Rinse again with cool water. Wipe rings outward from center with white cloth.
If stain persists or if fabric is delicate, call a professional cleaner.
